chapters.title: Many people are unhappy at work because there is a gap between what they do and who they are
chapters.text:
For many employees, offices are places where tasks get done and nothing else. But this is a wrong approach to creating a good work environment, and it’s particularly telling on workers in the tech industry.

Organizations need socialization to handle complicated production issues because ideas and innovation drive today’s progress. However, individuals are encouraged to use their wits to work nowadays and solve challenges that sometimes require team efforts. But this stifles creativity and promotes unhealthy rivalry. So, organizations need to develop new methods to produce valuable incentives to avoid this and encourage the maximization of skills. Unfortunately, few managers pause to consider the ramifications of this new reality, especially when creating a work climate that fosters individual growth and organizational success.

Collectivist workplace cultures make it easier to prioritize the group’s needs, objectives, and goals above the individual’s wants.

However, it is not enough to recruit talented, driven individuals to genuinely prosper in a world where innovation is the difference between success and failure. Skillful and well-meaning individuals can’t always get it right, especially when it comes to teamwork. Sometimes they don’t see the need to share information because they don’t want to stand out, be incorrect, or insult their employer. However, employees must feel comfortable sharing their knowledge, skills, or ideas to thrive in their workplaces.

This summary shares some methods to help create a functional and collaborative organization, intending to help you improve knowledge-intensive companies by providing new ideas and methods.

chapters.title: People tend to contribute more when they are sure their voices will be heard
chapters.text:
The world needs workplaces where individuals can express themselves without fear of retribution and ridicule.

Consider how companies in the creative or information sectors may profit from having access to everyone’s ideas. Hospitals, for example, could save many lives if nurses felt safe and obligated to question other hospital staff and physicians politely. Likewise, the United States could have prevented the Columbia and Challenger space shuttle mishaps if junior technicians felt comfortable voicing their opinions and psychologically safe.

Psychological safety means individuals feel secure expressing and being themselves; this should be every company’s goal. People feel morally obligated to speak up, share their expertise and opinions, and provide constructive criticism where their perspectives are valuable. This accelerates employee learning and information exchange. Most organizations talk about psychological safety but don't provide it, making it difficult to take risks, exchange ideas, and make sound decisions.

Good things happen when individuals speak out, ask questions, argue passionately, and commit to ongoing learning and growth.

Accepting new ideas — and recognizing and rewarding individuals for expressing their perspectives and pointing out errors — helps businesses cultivate courageous cultures. Psychological safety does not imply resistance to bad feedback or even job loss. Instead, it fosters an environment where individuals feel free to express themselves without fear of rejection, reprisal, or other negative consequences.

Google once analyzed 180 teams where members’ personalities, education, and abilities were considered. However, none of these characteristics satisfactorily explained the differences in team performance. The researchers, nonetheless, observed that although good teams have clear goals, meaningful work, and trustworthy teammates, the most important component present was psychological safety. While the teams with high psychological safety seemed to make more mistakes, they were the most productive. And although they made many mistakes, they avoided repeated errors, which encouraged future improvement.

chapters.title: Asking questions not only gives us a chance to know more but helps create a safe space for others
chapters.text:
People often remain silent even though they possess critical facts or views. They usually refrain from speaking out since they are worried that others will see them as people who delay progress or that speaking up would jeopardize their professional connections. Their grounds for not saying anything vary from wanting to avoid embarrassment to believing that their voices might be insignificant.

Overcoming ingrained psychological barriers lets people bring their whole selves to work.

Whether you are aware of it, you conduct daily risk assessments that decide whether you will ask questions or express your ideas. Most times, your worry of coming out as stupid or pessimistic may keep you silent. In most enterprises, being quiet carries fewer dangers. However, staying silent is detrimental to your psychological health and, perhaps, your company’s future.

Silence breeds fear, and fear suffocates security, inhibits thought and learning, keeps people silent, and obstructs the information flow required for knowledge enterprises to compete. Autocratic leadership mostly leads to short-term success. While fear may force employees to be at their best, it's not enough to sustain a high level of productivity. For instance, Volkswagen (VW) rose to become the world’s biggest maker of cars under Martin Winterkorn’s autocratic leadership. Yet, the employees were so scared that they habitually withheld wonderful ideas and negative news. While fear motivates, it also drives people to achieve objectives by whatever means necessary, which may be good for the short-term, but it doesn’t work well in the long run. For example, employees and executives at Volkswagen breached the law rather than speak up. As a result, the expenses in terms of penalties and reputational damage incurred because of Volkswagen’s 2015 diesel emissions crisis significantly outweighed any rewards.

Similarly, Wells Fargo pressed its staff to promote several banking services. Employees were under immense pressure to meet up with unrealistic expectations. As a result, workers created fictitious accounts and lied to clients, resulting in controversy and hefty penalties in 2016.

Allowing employees to ask questions makes them more comfortable and keeps any company from failing or experiencing financial crises. As was the case with Volkswagen and Wells Fargo, the implicit message was achieving success at any cost. But this didn't work out in the end, as VW retrenched or publicly humiliated underperformers, and although Wells Fargo’s first achievement seemed magical, it turned illusory.

chapters.title: It’s important to pay attention to details to avoid full-blown disasters
chapters.text:
Minor and often overlooked mistakes sometimes lead to huge challenges that spiral out of control. Nokia controlled the mobile business in 2000 and was set to pioneer the smartphone world. The company’s engineers knew of Apple and Samsung’s advancements, but their arrogant attitude caused engineers and managers to pretend all was well. Their feigned ignorance resulted in a 75% decline in Nokia’s shares and the sale of a large portion of the company to Microsoft in 2013.

This same issue of ignorance led to the space shuttle Challenger’s explosion shortly after takeoff in 1986, killing everyone on board. This happened despite NASA engineer Roger Boisjoly’s warnings that a possible defect in the shuttle’s O-rings in cold weather exists.

Similarly, when the Columbia space shuttle was destroyed upon re-entry into the Earth’s atmosphere in 2003, hardly anyone listened to repeated warnings. Engineer Rodney Rocha had expressed his worries about the heat shield to his employer, but his boss dismissed him. Going over his manager’s head was frowned upon in Rocha’s culture, so he remained silent. When the heat shield eventually failed, seven astronauts lost their lives.

You realize that success and failure are equally significant to growth when you take chances.

The workplace culture of silence is potent, especially in environments that encourage people to keep their ideas in their minds; no one wants to risk pushing views on a boss who seems to know everything. Cultures built on fear or emphasizing seniority and authority must change so that individuals will feel secure breaking their silence. Building a truly open and courageous company will take time and effort, but your company will profit as individuals exchange ideas, ask questions, and learn.

Psychological safety emerges in teams or groups, not throughout an organization. Therefore, removing fear as a motivator increases people's confidence in themselves.

Leadership at its core is about harnessing others' efforts to achieve something no one can achieve alone. ~ Amy C. Edmondson

As a leader, you should assess existing psychological safety by educating your staff on the dangers of not speaking out. Include questions regarding team members’ comfort in sharing ideas and discussing errors, obstacles, and sensitive subjects. Use a five- or seven-point Likert scale (examples available on the Internet), a psychometric measure often used in survey research.

chapters.title: Leaders ought to help people see mistakes and criticism as chances for growth
chapters.text:
Your task as a team lead is to remind others that you are aware of the complexity of their functions and that you anticipate difficulties and errors. Demonstrate how everyone’s job impacts the obligations of others and explain why regular communication, knowledge sharing, and openness are necessary. Emphasize that remaining quiet might be detrimental to the company and jeopardize employment or lives because withheld information can make a huge difference. So, point out mistakes or errors in ways that won’t make employees feel bad or less of themselves. Constructive criticism goes a long way in helping people make better decisions in the workplace. Some leaders believe that being blunt or instilling fear in workers improves them, but this assumption is far from true. While fear may force employees to be extra careful, it makes them less productive and hardly creative.

The financial firm Bridgewater Associates requires employees to collaborate on projects. Failure to do so violates the firm’s ideals and, in the opinion of founder Ray Dalio, constitutes unethical conduct.

Engage participants and ask workers regularly if they have any suggestions, concerns, questions, or comments. Attend to them rather than wait for them to come to you and inquire how the business can improve its processes to make them safer and more transparent. Demonstrate humility as a leader to convey your desire to know, be ethically vulnerable and encourage collaboration and innovative thinking. It instills trust in workers and lets you tap into their combined intellect, views, and expertise. This is important because teamwork is crucial for success, and employees always want to feel heard and contribute ideas.

Silence keeps us safe but makes us underperform and unsatisfied.

When individuals speak out, encourage their bravery by listening intently. Recognize and appreciate those who speak up as this gives others the courage to do so in the future. Demonstrate your gratitude for their feedback and take action, even if it means clarifying why their suggestions will fail.

The courage it takes to speak up must receive the mini-reward of thanks. ~ Amy C. Edmondson

Did you know? According to 2021 research by the University of Warwick, employees’ productivity increased by 12% when they got feedback directly from their manager.

chapters.title: If there’s no commitment to team cohesion, efforts to encourage employees to speak out won’t work
chapters.text:
One of the easiest ways to get people on board is to lead by example. If a manager is open-minded and interested in the personal growth of their employees, it becomes easy for the workers to emulate such a leader. For example, in 2007, CEO Cynthia Carroll shut down Anglo American’s highest-producing yet most-dangerous mine to talk to employees about reducing accidents and fatalities. Before her, Anglo’s culture forbade miners from disagreeing or questioning orders, even if they felt it was dangerous. Carroll won employees’ confidence and persuaded them to share their thoughts. By 2011, mining fatalities had reduced by almost 60%, and Anglo became profitable.

The Fukushima accident had a similar situation. Naohiro Masuda, the plant supervisor at Fukushima Daini, a nuclear plant site, used a whiteboard to guide his workers through a tsunami. He offered the crew as much information as possible in a rapidly changing situation. This attitude is vital for achieving a goal that requires creativity and ingenuity. The men ended up doing in 24 hours what would have taken 30 men a month to accomplish, making it easier to escape danger.

Junior employees should be coached even when they criticize senior executives’ judgments. It’s normal to expect inquiries from subordinates, peers, and superiors since questions allow individuals to justify their actions and decisions. It’s also vital to adopt a state of ignorance to be more receptive to other people’s opinions by rewarding those who strive hard yet still fail. Google’s experimental “X” division promotes early failure and project abandonment to concentrate resources on the most viable ideas. Through public recognition and rewards, Google promotes its ideals of appropriate risk-taking and safety in failure. It stops team members from concealing facts and wasting time and money on failed initiatives. Discussions on project failures should be transparent and courteous. Thus, speaking out is safer, and errors are less likely to be repeated.

Encouraging workers to coach and educate one another helps with idea exchanges and skills that work well for the organization.

No one succeeds by being right all the time, so it’s essential to address those who, even in speaking out, go against our established and unambiguous beliefs. Organizations that care about their people establish trust. Creating psychological safety inside a talent culture enhances its effect because combining bravery initiatives with employee engagement leads to learning, development, and inclusivity.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The absence of personal interactions in the office often results in a significant decrease in employee productivity. There is a disconnect between people’s inner perception of a world and the reality in which they live, which is particularly true as many people are dissatisfied with their work. Every firm needs to foster workplace connections that stimulate cooperation and exchange of ideas among its employees. When individuals collaborate, they are more likely to produce more significant results.

While team leaders shape workplace expectations and behaviors, anyone can help create psychological safety. Sometimes all it takes is a good question. An excellent question is driven by genuine curiosity or a desire to empower others. Questions create openings where someone may speak out.

Building an open and fearless organization isn’t an option; it’s a requirement for a healthy, successful business. A fearless organization needs great connections from the very top of the hierarchical chain all the way down to the bottom. When individuals feel free to express themselves, it opens the door to more benefits and improvement. Unfortunately, many CEOs and other executives tend to abdicate their duties, resulting in a lack of understanding of what is going on. This strategy may be effective in the short term, but in the long run, workers may feel uninspired and unclear about their role in the organization’s overall success.

Try this

As a team leader, be open to receiving suggestions, ideas, and feedback from your employees. A suggestion box or an anonymous survey can help you know more about your workers' thoughts and feelings, giving you better insight and making problem-solving easier. Make team meetings necessary and promote a strong connection among workers by encouraging team assignments and group tasks.

Read it till the end?
